subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content:
#### Player-Count Reference (Step 0 Audit)
- **Standard game** — 2 to 4 players.
- **Recommended** — 2 players for competitive play.

#### Board
- **Game board** — 1 folding board with a 15x15 grid (225 squares).
- **Premium squares** — Double Letter Score (light blue), Triple Letter Score (dark blue), Double Word Score (pink), Triple Word Score (red).
- **Center star** — The center square is marked with a star and counts as a Double Word Score.

#### Tiles
- **Letter tiles** — 100 tiles total.
- **Letter distribution** — Weighted by English frequency: E x12, A x9, I x9, O x8, N x6, R x6, T x6, L x4, S x4, U x4, D x4, G x3, and so on.
- **Blank tiles** — 2 blank tiles (wild — represent any letter, worth 0 points).
- **Point values** — Printed on each tile. 1 pt: E, A, I, O, N, R, T, L, S, U. 2 pt: D, G. 3 pt: B, C, M, P. 4 pt: F, H, V, W, Y. 5 pt: K. 8 pt: J, X. 10 pt: Q, Z.

#### Other Components
- **Tile bag** — 1 drawstring cloth bag.
- **Tile racks** — 4 wooden or plastic letter racks (1 per player).
- **Rulebook** — 1 rulebook.

subtopics.title: Board Layout
subtopics.content:
#### Board Grid
- **15x15 grid** — 225 total squares arranged in a symmetric pattern.
- **Center star** — The exact center square (row 8, column 8) has a star. It is a Double Word Score square.

#### Premium Square Layout (Symmetric)
- **Triple Word Score (red)** — 8 squares, located at the four corners and mid-edges of the board.
- **Double Word Score (pink)** — 17 squares, forming diagonal lines from the corners toward the center.
- **Triple Letter Score (dark blue)** — 12 squares, scattered in a symmetric pattern.
- **Double Letter Score (light blue)** — 24 squares, scattered in a symmetric pattern.
- **Regular squares** — Remaining squares have no bonus.

#### Tile Bag
- **Central draw pile** — Place all 100 tiles in the bag and mix thoroughly. The bag stays accessible to all players.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content:
#### Starting Draw
- **Rack** — Each player takes 1 tile rack.
- **Initial tiles** — Each player draws 7 tiles from the bag without looking, places them on their rack.
- **Hidden information** — Players keep their rack tiles hidden from opponents.

#### Determining First Player
- **Draw method** — Each player draws 1 tile from the bag. Closest to 'A' goes first (blank beats all).
- **Return and redraw** — Return those tiles to the bag, remix, then each player draws their 7 starting tiles.
- **Turn order** — Play proceeds clockwise from the starting player.

subtopics.title: Starting the Game
subtopics.content:
#### First Word
- **Center star** — The first word must be placed so that one letter covers the center star.
- **Direction** — The first word must be placed either horizontally (left to right) or vertically (top to bottom).
- **Minimum length** — The first word must be at least 2 letters.
- **Double Word Score** — Because the center star is a Double Word Score, the first word's total letter value is doubled.

#### After First Word
- **Draw to 7** — The first player draws tiles from the bag to refill their rack to 7 tiles.
- **Next player** — Play passes clockwise.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
#### Turn Options (Choose One)
1. **Play a word** — Place 1 or more tiles on the board to form a new valid word, then draw back to 7 tiles.
2. **Exchange tiles** — Swap any number of tiles from your rack for new tiles from the bag (must have 7+ tiles in bag). This uses your entire turn; you score 0 points.
3. **Pass** — Score 0 points and change nothing. Play moves to the next player.

#### Word Placement Rules
- **Single line** — All tiles placed in one turn must be in a single row or single column.
- **Connected** — At least one tile must be adjacent to (and form a word with) an existing tile on the board (except the first word).
- **All words valid** — Every new horizontal and vertical word formed by the placement must be a valid dictionary word.
- **No gaps** — Tiles placed in a row/column must be contiguous (existing board tiles may fill gaps).

#### After Playing
- **Score the turn** — Calculate points for all newly formed words this turn.
- **Draw tiles** — Draw from the bag until you have 7 tiles (or the bag is empty).
- **Next player** — Play passes clockwise.

subtopics.title: Actions & Options
subtopics.content:
#### Scoring a Word
- **Base value** — Sum the point values of all letters in each newly formed word.
- **Letter premiums** — If a tile is placed on a Double Letter Score, that tile's value is doubled. Triple Letter Score triples it.
- **Word premiums** — If a tile is placed on a Double Word Score, the entire word's value is doubled after letter premiums. Triple Word Score triples the entire word.
- **Multiple premiums** — If a word covers two word-premium squares, multiply accordingly (e.g., double then triple = x6).
- **Premium applies once** — Premium squares only count on the turn the tile is first placed on them. They have no effect on subsequent turns.
- **Blank tiles** — Always worth 0 points, even on premium squares.

#### Bingo Bonus
- **All 7 tiles** — If a player uses all 7 tiles from their rack in a single turn, they earn a **50-point bonus** on top of the word score.

#### Challenging a Word
- **Challenge** — After a word is played (before the next player's turn), any player may challenge its validity.
- **Dictionary check** — Look up the word in the agreed-upon dictionary.
- **Invalid word** — The player removes their tiles, scores 0 for that turn, and loses their turn.
- **Valid word** — The challenger loses their next turn (in tournament rules). House rules may vary.

#### Blank Tiles
- **Wildcard** — A blank tile can represent any letter. The player declares the letter when placing it.
- **Permanent** — Once placed, a blank remains that letter for the rest of the game.
- **Worth 0** — Blank tiles always score 0 points.

subtopics.title: Special Mechanics
subtopics.content:
#### Tile Exchange
- **Minimum bag requirement** — At least 7 tiles must remain in the bag to exchange.
- **Process** — Place unwanted tiles face-down, draw the same number of new tiles, then mix the old tiles back into the bag.
- **Full turn cost** — You cannot play a word and exchange in the same turn.

#### Valid Words
- **Dictionary** — Use an agreed-upon dictionary (Official Scrabble Players Dictionary for North America, Collins Scrabble Words internationally).
- **Allowed** — Any word in the official word list, including archaic, obscure, and informal words.
- **Not allowed** — Proper nouns, abbreviations, prefixes/suffixes standing alone, hyphenated words, words requiring apostrophes.

#### Parallel Word Formation
- **Cross-words** — Placing tiles parallel to an existing word can form multiple 2-letter words simultaneously. All must be valid.
- **Hooks** — Adding a single letter to the front or back of an existing word (e.g., adding S to CART to make CARTS) while also forming a perpendicular word.

#### Two-Letter Words
- **Strategic importance** — The official word list contains many valid 2-letter words (AA, AB, AD, AE, AG, AH, AI, AL, AM, AN, etc.). Knowledge of these is essential for advanced play.

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content:
#### Endgame Trigger
- **Bag empty + tiles out** — The game ends when the tile bag is empty AND one player places their last tile. That player "goes out."
- **All players pass** — The game also ends if all players pass consecutively (no one can or wants to play).

#### Endgame Scoring Adjustment
- **Going-out bonus** — The player who goes out adds the total value of all opponents' remaining rack tiles to their score.
- **Remaining tile penalty** — Each other player subtracts the total value of their own unplayed rack tiles from their score.

#### Winning
- **Highest score wins** — The player with the highest cumulative score wins.
- **Tiebreaker** — If tied, the player with the highest score before the endgame adjustment wins.

#### Scoring Model
- **Score sources** — Word scores (letter values + premium multipliers), bingo bonuses (50 pts each), endgame adjustment.
- **Typical scores** — Casual games: 200-350 points. Competitive games: 350-500+ points.

subtopics.title: Opening Priorities
subtopics.content:
#### Tile Management
- **Balance your rack** — Aim for a mix of vowels and consonants. Ideal ratio is roughly 3 consonants to 2 vowels with flexible tiles.
- **Keep high-value tiles accessible** — Hold S, blank, and common letters for bingo opportunities.
- **Dump duplicates** — If you have 3 of the same letter, exchange or play to improve rack composition.

#### Board Position
- **Open vs closed** — Playing toward premium squares opens scoring for you but also for opponents. Balance offensive and defensive placement.
- **First word** — Maximize the center Double Word Score. A 4-5 letter word using a high-value letter is ideal.

subtopics.title: Common Mistakes
subtopics.content:
#### Frequent Errors
- **Playing the S cheaply** — The S tile is extremely valuable for hooking onto existing words. Never use it for fewer than 10+ extra points.
- **Ignoring 2-letter words** — Not knowing valid 2-letter words severely limits placement options and parallel plays.
- **Chasing the Triple Word** — Forcing a weak word onto a Triple Word Score when a stronger word elsewhere scores more.
- **Hoarding blanks** — Holding a blank too long waiting for a bingo. If no bingo materializes in 2-3 turns, use it.
- **Opening the board recklessly** — Placing tiles near Triple Word squares without blocking gives opponents easy high-scoring opportunities.
- **Forgetting cross-words** — When placing tiles, failing to verify that all newly formed perpendicular words are valid.

subtopics.title: Key Decisions
subtopics.content:
#### Decision 1: Play or Exchange?
- **Play** — If you can score 20+ points, generally play rather than exchange.
- **Exchange** — If your rack is severely imbalanced (all vowels, all consonants, or Q without U) and no decent word exists, exchange.

#### Decision 2: Offensive vs Defensive
- **Offensive** — Open the board toward Triple Word squares when you can reach them next turn.
- **Defensive** — Block premium squares when your opponent is likely to exploit them. Close down open lanes.

#### Decision 3: Bingo Hunting
- **Push for bingo** — When you have 5-6 good tiles and 1-2 blanks, invest a turn to exchange the weak tiles and aim for the 50-point bonus.
- **Take the points** — When the board is tight or the bag is nearly empty, reliable points beat speculative bingo attempts.

#### Decision 4: Endgame Tile Management
- **Go out first** — In the endgame, prioritize emptying your rack to gain the going-out bonus and avoid the penalty.
- **Avoid high-value leftovers** — Q (10 pts) and Z (10 pts) stuck on your rack at game end is a 20-point swing against you.
